pair_convention.spot_explanation: CAD/CHF at 0.5683 means 1 Canadian Dollar buys 0.5683 Swiss Francs. A rising pair means CAD is strengthening against CHF (more CHF per CAD); a falling pair means CAD is weakening against CHF (fewer CHF per CAD). The pair has been in a persistent downtrend, falling from 0.6254 in early March 2025 to a low of 0.5595 in October 2025, currently sitting at the 13th percentile of its 52-week range.

executive_summary: CAD/CHF trades at 0.5683 as of May 9, 2026, sitting at the 13th percentile of its 52-week range (0.5595–0.6254), having declined 3.52% over the past year and 1.55% over six months. The pair encapsulates a classic commodity-currency versus safe-haven-currency tension, pitting Canada's oil-driven terms-of-trade windfall (+200bps carry advantage via BoC 2.25% vs SNB 0.00%) against Switzerland's structural current-account surplus (~7% of GDP), best-in-class fiscal position (debt/GDP 16.1%), and uniquely low inflation (0.6% headline, 0.3% core). The bilateral narrative is dominated by two opposing forces: CAD benefits from WTI above $100/bbl and a hawkish BoC hold, while CHF benefits from safe-haven demand on the Iran war and its status as the cheapest real-yield currency in G10 stagflation. Our tactical stance is Bearish CAD/CHF (pair falls) at conviction 2/5: the carry advantage for CAD is insufficient to overcome CHF's safe-haven magnetism and structural surplus over the next 2–4 weeks, with the USMCA review creating additional CAD-specific headwind. Our strategic stance is also Bearish CAD/CHF at conviction 2/5: while carry compression (Fed cutting, BoC holding) and oil tailwinds gradually support CAD, CHF's structural appreciation trend and fiscal quality dominance are likely to persist, leaving the pair in a slow grind lower. The net weighted tactical score is -0.130 and the strategic score is -0.259, both mapping to low-conviction bearish with 9 of 10 factors neutral or bearish for the pair at the strategic horizon.

market_context.valuation_assessment: The CAD dossier notes CAD is approximately 31% undervalued vs CHF on a PPP basis (CAD/CHF PPP fair value ~0.81 vs spot 0.57). The CHF dossier confirms CHF is 10–14% overvalued vs CAD. This is among the most extreme PPP dislocations in the G10 cross-pair universe but has been persistent for years, reflecting Switzerland's structural current-account surplus and low-inflation regime. Valuation does not provide a tactical signal but sets a strategic floor: the pair is unlikely to fall below 0.50 without a fundamental regime change. The pair has been below PPP for over a decade, limiting PPP's tactical utility.

positioning.base_cftc_net: -54,703 contracts (leveraged funds net short); asset managers net long +15,528
positioning.quote_cftc_net: -37,534 contracts (asset managers net short); leveraged funds net short -5,174
positioning.net_pair_implication: The market is short CAD (via leveraged funds) AND short CHF (via asset managers), producing an ambiguous net pair signal. However, the CHF asset-manager short is at a more extreme percentile (95th vs CAD leveraged fund 70th), suggesting greater squeeze potential on the CHF side — which would drive CHF higher and CAD/CHF lower. The combined positioning implies no clear directional edge but modestly favours CHF strength on squeeze dynamics.
positioning.crowding_assessment: Offsetting squeeze potentials; CHF squeeze at more extreme percentile (95th) slightly dominates

pair_technicals.key_moving_averages: 50d SMA at 0.5730, 100d SMA at 0.5710, 200d SMA at 0.5723 — all above spot 0.5683; bearishly stacked configuration (spot below all three MAs, 50d below 200d), confirming downtrend regime
pair_technicals.chart_pattern: Descending channel from April 2025 intact; failed rally from 0.5784 (April 28) to 0.5683 (May 9) — 100-pip decline in 10 days confirms sellers dominate rallies. The 0.5765 zone does significant technical heavy lifting: pivot point resistance, 61.8% Fibonacci retracement, and near the top of the descending channel.

pair_volatility.rv_vs_iv: Realised vol (5.5–6.5% annualised) is approximately in line with estimated implied vol (6–7%), suggesting no significant variance risk premium in either direction; the pair is a low-vol cross reflecting slow-moving structural dynamics
pair_volatility.risk_reversals: Not directly available from public sources for this cross; estimated to be modestly skewed toward CHF calls (downside CAD/CHF puts) given the safe-haven premium and bearish trend
pair_volatility.vol_term_structure: Flat to slightly elevated for near-term events (Canada CPI May 19, USMCA July 1); the pair's average daily range of 40–50 pips is consistent with a low-volatility grind environment with episodic spikes around geopolitical events
pair_volatility.vol_regime: Low vol / compressing — pair in 40–50 pip daily range grind; CAD/CHF volatility rating approximately 0.51%

market_context.recent_regime: CAD/CHF has been consolidating in a 0.5640–0.5790 range since early March 2026, following a recovery from the January-February 2026 lows near 0.5600. The recent 20 trading days show a failure to sustain above the 50d SMA (0.5730) — the pair briefly rallied to 0.5784 on April 28–29 before reversing sharply to 0.5683. The dominant narrative is carry-versus-haven: oil above $100 supports CAD, but the Iran war and Swiss safe-haven flows keep CHF bid, compressing the cross into an increasingly narrow range with a bearish tilt.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move approximately 65 pips (1.14%) against before carry is erased over 6M; for the tactical 30-day window, only 10 pips of carry income are earned, meaning pair must not decline more than 10 pips for carry to be positive

forward_points.1m: -12 pips (CAD at forward premium vs CHF due to positive rate differential)

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The BoC-SNB policy rate differential of +225bps makes CAD/CHF one of the more attractive carry longs among CHF crosses, with a carry-to-vol ratio of +0.35 — the best in the CHF cross complex. The 2Y spread is approximately +275bps (Canada 2.85% vs Switzerland ~0.10%) and the 10Y spread is +325bps (Canada 3.60% vs Switzerland 0.35%). However, the carry regime is currently suppressed: carry-trade strategies have been consistently unprofitable in CHF pairs since the Iran war began, as safe-haven flows overwhelm the rate differential. Over a 30-day tactical horizon, carry income of approximately 10 pips is negligible relative to the pair's daily range of 40–50 pips. Over 6 months, carry income of approximately 65 pips sets the hurdle rate for a profitable short position — our base-case target of 0.5550 implies a 133-pip decline, comfortably exceeding the carry cost. The 1M forward outright is approximately 0.5671, 3M ~0.5649, and 6M ~0.5617, with the forward curve (driven by the positive differential) pricing CAD at a persistent forward premium vs CHF. The optimal strategic expression for a bearish CAD/CHF view is therefore a forward sale, which captures the forward premium (forward points work in seller's favour) rather than absorbing negative carry in a spot short.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The BoC-SNB rate differential of +225bps is the widest bilateral policy gap available for any CHF cross pair except AUD/CHF (+435bps) and NOK/CHF (+425bps). The BoC held at 2.25% on April 29 with hawkish-hold language, while the SNB held at 0.00% on March 19 with intervention rhetoric. Both central banks are expected to hold through their next meetings (BoC June 10, SNB June 18), meaning the rate gap is stable over the tactical window. At the 2Y point, Canada's 2.85% vs Switzerland's estimated 0.10% delivers +275bps of front-end carry that flows through to forward points. The OIS-implied path shows neither bank moving in the 2-4 week window. The carry-to-vol ratio of +0.35 is the best in the CHF cross complex, making CAD/CHF the most carry-efficient long CHF short among G10 pairs. However, the Iran ceasefire binary creates near-term path-dependency: ceasefire success would validate carry trades (CAD/CHF higher); failure/re-escalation would crush them (CHF safe-haven bid dominates).
key_bilateral_metrics: BoC rate: 2.25% vs SNB rate: 0.00% = +225bps differential
Canada 2Y: 2.85% vs Switzerland 2Y: ~0.10% = +275bps
Canada 10Y: 3.60% vs Switzerland 10Y: 0.35% = +325bps
Carry-to-vol ratio: +0.35 (most favourable CHF cross)
Next meetings: BoC June 10 (hold), SNB June 18 (hold)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price both banks on hold with no movement through Q3 2026. The +225bps gap is fully embedded in forward points. No tactical mispricing exists in the rate differential itself — the edge is in whether carry can overcome safe-haven flows.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the rate differential trajectory is mildly CAD/CHF-positive. The BoC is on hold with hawkish bias (potential hike Q1 2027), while the SNB faces conditional risk of cutting to -0.25% if EUR/CHF breaks 0.90 and medium-term inflation undershoots. If the SNB cuts to negative rates, the bilateral gap widens from +225bps to +250bps, mechanically supporting CAD/CHF. The Fed-cut trajectory (25-50bps by year-end) indirectly supports CAD via carry compression of the CA-US spread but does not directly affect CAD/CHF. The BoJ hiking trajectory narrows the CHF-JPY gap more than it affects the CAD-CHF gap. Over 6 months, the carry-to-vol ratio may improve if realised vol compresses post-Iran-resolution, making the carry leg more dominant. The strategic risk is that the SNB does NOT cut to negative rates (base case ~75-85%), keeping the gap stable rather than widening.
key_bilateral_metrics: Expected end-2026 BoC: 2.25-2.50% vs SNB: 0.00% to -0.25%
Carry gap trajectory: stable to widening (+225 to +250bps)
Forward-implied 6M CAD/CHF: ~0.5617 (carry premium for longs)
BoC-SNB OIS 6M forward: BoC 2.25%, SNB ~0.00%
SNB negative-rate probability: ~25-40% by year-end
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price the rate gap as stable. We see modest widening risk if SNB cuts — currently underpriced.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons Bullish CAD/CHF on carry, with tactical confidence higher (60 vs 55) because the 2-4 week carry is cleaner (no rate changes imminent), while the 6M horizon introduces SNB action uncertainty.

carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move approximately 65 pips against before carry is erased over 6M

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The inflation-growth differential is the most striking bilateral contrast in this pair. Canada's headline CPI at 2.4% dwarfs Switzerland's 0.6%, yet Canada's core measures (~2.3%) are far above Switzerland's core (~0.3%). This means CHF holders earn a higher real yield: Swiss real 10Y at approximately -0.25% vs Canada's real 10Y at approximately +1.20% — but the framing is inverted for CAD/CHF because the market rewards Switzerland's low-inflation structural premium as a store-of-value. Canada's Q4 2025 GDP contracted -0.6% while Switzerland grew +0.2% — both weak but Canada is demonstrably softer. Canada's unemployment at 6.7% is the highest in G10, more than double Switzerland's 3.0%. The April 2026 CPI release on May 19 is a key bilateral catalyst: a hot Canadian print (2.7%) would support CAD via hawkish BoC repricing; a soft Swiss CPI print (<0.5%) would intensify CHF real-yield premium. The data surprise differential favours CHF: Swiss PMI at 54.5 (above expectations) vs Canada's stalling Q1 GDP creates a growth-momentum gap.
key_bilateral_metrics: Canada CPI 2.4% vs Switzerland CPI 0.6% = 180bps inflation gap
Canada core ~2.3% vs Switzerland core ~0.3% = 200bps core gap
Canada Q4 GDP -0.6% vs Switzerland Q4 GDP +0.2%
Canada unemployment 6.7% vs Switzerland 3.0%
Swiss PMI 54.5 (highest since Oct 2022) vs Canada Q1 stalling
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price Canadian CPI drifting higher on energy base effects and Swiss CPI staying anchored at 0.5-0.7%. The underpriced dynamic is Switzerland's disinflationary feedback loop: CHF strength → import deflation → lower CPI → more real-yield support → more CHF strength. This self-reinforcing mechanism is CAD/CHF-bearish.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Canada's stagflationary mix (rising energy CPI, weak growth, mortgage renewal stress) contrasts with Switzerland's benign environment (low stable inflation, moderate growth, no household balance sheet stress). Over 6 months, the mortgage renewal wave affecting 60% of Canadian mortgages with +6% average payment increases compresses consumer spending, while Swiss household balance sheets face no equivalent stress. The 2026 GDP forecasts are BoC 1.2% vs SECO 1.0% — similar headline levels but Canada has more downside risk from trade policy (USMCA) while Switzerland has more upside from pharma exports and gold refining. The inflation differential is structurally CHF-positive: Switzerland's permanently lower inflation (~0.5% long-run) vs Canada's higher trend (~2.0-2.5%) has been the foundation of the CHF appreciation trend for decades.
key_bilateral_metrics: 2026 GDP forecast: Canada 1.2% vs Switzerland 1.0%
Mortgage renewal stress: Canada 60% cohort 2025-26; Switzerland nil
Inflation structural trend: Canada ~2.0-2.5% vs Switzerland ~0.5%
Household debt/income: Canada 176.7% vs Switzerland ~140% (lower)
Growth surprise momentum: Swiss PMI 54.5 vs Canada stalling
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price similar growth paths. The underpriced risk is Canadian mortgage stress translating to consumer retreat in H2 2026, dropping growth toward 0.8% — this would weigh on CAD/CHF.

factors.horizon_split: Both Bearish CAD/CHF; confidence similar. The structural inflation differential is the dominant strategic driver.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Pure risk-proxy pair (commodity vs safe-haven); risk factor deserves higher weight. Weight boosted +0.03 tactically (0.10 to 0.13) and +0.03 strategically (0.05 to 0.08).

tactical.bilateral_analysis: CAD/CHF is a classic risk-on/risk-off pair: CAD is a commodity currency sensitive to global growth outlook while CHF is a safety-geared, low-yielding safe haven. The pair's beta to risk appetite is among the highest in G10 — historically, CAD/CHF falls 3-5% during significant risk-off episodes. The current environment (VIX at 17, equities near highs, oil $100) is constructive but the active Iran ceasefire negotiation creates binary near-term risk. If ceasefire fails and the Iran war re-escalates, CHF would strengthen sharply against CAD as safe-haven flows accelerate — a 3-5% CAD/CHF decline is plausible. Conversely, ceasefire success would trigger safe-haven unwind and CAD/CHF rally. Gold at $4,700+ reinforces the safe-haven premium currently supporting CHF. The risk-sentiment channel is asymmetric against CAD/CHF: the downside tail (re-escalation) is larger than the upside tail (ceasefire), because safe-haven unwinds are typically slower than safe-haven builds.
key_bilateral_metrics: VIX: 17.13 (35th percentile of 1Y range)
Gold: $4,700+ (safe-haven premium embedded)
WTI: $104/bbl (supports CAD but also signals geopolitical stress)
CAD/CHF beta to VIX: approximately -0.20 to -0.30
Iran ceasefire probability: ~50/50 per market pricing
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price a benign risk environment. The asymmetric downside risk from Iran re-escalation is modestly underpriced for CAD/CHF.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, multiple risk-off catalysts favour CHF: Iran re-escalation risk, USMCA breakdown risk, US-China tariff risk, Eurozone fiscal stress, US debt ceiling. The cumulative probability of at least one major risk-off event over 6 months is approximately 50%. Each would disproportionately benefit CHF vs CAD given the pair's high risk-beta. Canada's USMCA-specific trade-policy risk adds an asymmetric CAD-negative dimension that CHF is insulated from. The TSX 1Y return of +36.68% also presents mean-reversion risk in H2 2026, which could weigh on risk-sensitive CAD.
key_bilateral_metrics: 6M cumulative risk-off probability: ~50%
CAD/CHF typical risk-off drawdown: 3-5%
TSX 1Y return: +36.68% (mean-reversion risk in H2)
SMI defensive composition supports CHF during equity vol
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price continuation of benign risk regime. Underpriced cumulative tail-risk exposure over 6M is mildly CAD/CHF-bearish.

factors.horizon_split: Both Bearish; tactical confidence higher (60 vs 50) because the binary Iran catalyst is immediate, while strategic risk-off exposure is more diffuse.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The pair-level positioning picture is ambiguous. On the CAD side, leveraged funds are net short -54,703 contracts (squeeze-prone for CAD strength = CAD/CHF bullish). On the CHF side, asset managers are net short -37,534 contracts at the 95th percentile of 5Y history (squeeze-prone for CHF strength = CAD/CHF bearish). These two squeeze potentials effectively offset: a CAD short squeeze would push CAD/CHF higher, but a CHF short squeeze would push it lower. The CHF squeeze potential is arguably larger given the more extreme positioning percentile (95th vs 70th). The pair-specific flow dynamic is carry-trade related: in benign risk environments, CAD/CHF attracts carry longs (positive for the pair); in risk-off, carry longs are unwound aggressively. With the Iran ceasefire binary, neither positioning extreme can be exploited with conviction. Net signal: Neutral.
key_bilateral_metrics: CAD lev fund net short: -54,703 (70th percentile; squeeze bullish CAD/CHF)
CHF asset manager net short: -37,534 (95th percentile; squeeze bearish CAD/CHF)
Net pair signal: offsetting squeeze potentials
Carry-trade community in CAD/CHF: moderate (less liquid than USD/CHF or EUR/CHF)
Pair open interest: relatively low — thin cross with gap risk
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price both positions as stable. The asymmetric squeeze risk slightly favours CHF (larger percentile extreme), but the difference is insufficient for a directional call.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the CHF asset-manager net short at the 95th percentile is more likely to normalise (slow unwind supporting CHF) than the CAD leveraged fund short (which depends on USMCA outcome). If USMCA resolves positively, CAD leveraged fund shorts unwind (CAD/CHF bullish), but the CHF normalisation also proceeds (CAD/CHF bearish). The net effect is modestly CAD/CHF-bearish because the CHF positioning is at a more extreme level and its normalisation is less event-dependent.
key_bilateral_metrics: CHF 6M positioning normalisation: ~15-20k contract reduction (CHF-positive)
CAD 6M positioning normalisation: USMCA-dependent
Pair-level net flow expectation: mildly CHF-positive
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets underweight CHF positioning normalisation speed in the cross.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral, Strategic mildly Bearish. The divergence reflects the CHF squeeze potential becoming more actionable at strategic horizons.

tactical.combined_positioning_signal: Market is short both CAD (leveraged funds at 70th percentile) and CHF (asset managers at 95th percentile), producing an ambiguous net pair signal with modestly greater CHF squeeze potential
tactical.unwind_trigger: Iran ceasefire confirmation + USMCA positive headline simultaneously would trigger CAD short unwind; risk-off escalation would trigger CHF short unwind — currently binary rather than trending
tactical.historical_analogue: October 2025 CHF asset-manager extreme short preceded the CAD/CHF rally from 0.5595 low — positioning-driven bounces historically last 3-4 weeks before structural factors reassert
tactical.contrarian_case: The 95th-percentile CHF asset-manager short is historically associated with a 2-4% CHF rally over the subsequent 4-8 weeks as positioning normalises. This would push CAD/CHF to 0.5490-0.5550 — the contrarian case is actually MORE bearish than the base case, reinforcing the short bias while flagging the risk of a temporary counter-squeeze toward 0.5780-0.5800 if the CAD short (70th percentile) unwinds first.

strategic.combined_positioning_signal: CHF asset-manager short at 95th percentile expected to normalise over 6M regardless of specific catalysts; CAD leveraged fund short normalisation is USMCA-contingent and therefore less predictable
strategic.unwind_trigger: USMCA resolution (positive = CAD unwind) or SNB negative-rate cut (CHF positioning reassessment)
strategic.historical_analogue: 2014-2015: CHF positioning extremes normalised over 6-12 months before the SNB floor removal; 2022: CHF asset-manager shorts at high percentiles normalised ahead of SNB surprise hike
strategic.contrarian_case: The more extreme CHF positioning (95th percentile) has historically normalised faster than CAD positioning (70th percentile), generating net CHF buying pressure over the strategic horizon. Even a partial CHF positioning normalisation of 10-15k contracts represents meaningful CHF demand that would mechanically push CAD/CHF lower by 50-100 pips independent of any fundamental catalyst.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: This factor requires careful netting of two commodity-exposed economies. Canada benefits directly from oil above $100/bbl (net energy exporter); Switzerland benefits from gold at $4,700+ (world's largest gold refiner, 65-70% of global refining). Oil is a positive for CAD but Switzerland's gold trade flows are running 25-30% above trend, partially offsetting. The current-account differential is the structural anchor: Canada's CA deficit (-1.1% of GDP, narrowing on oil) vs Switzerland's CA surplus (+7% of GDP, ~CHF 70bn/yr) creates a persistent structural flow advantage for CHF. Over 2-4 weeks, the CA flow is too slow to dominate spot; oil price moves are faster. Two-way merchandise trade between Switzerland and Canada amounted to $14.3 billion in 2024, with Switzerland running a bilateral goods surplus — Swiss pharma/watch exports exceed Canadian commodity exports to Switzerland. This bilateral flow modestly supports CHF on a structural basis.
key_bilateral_metrics: Canada CA/GDP: -1.1% vs Switzerland CA/GDP: +7.0% = -810bps gap
WTI $104/bbl: ++CAD positive; Gold $4,700: +CHF positive
Net commodity exposure: oil benefits CAD more than gold benefits CHF, but CA surplus overwhelms
Swiss exports to Canada: ~$8 billion vs Canadian exports to Switzerland: ~$6.3 billion
Switzerland is Canada's 8th-most important source of FDI; Swiss FDI stock in Canada: $24 billion
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price oil as a temporary tailwind for CAD while Swiss surplus flows continue as structural CHF support. No clear tactical mispricing.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the structural current-account differential dominates. Switzerland's ~CHF 70bn/yr (~$80bn) surplus generates approximately CHF 35bn of net inflows over the 6M horizon — equivalent to ~10% of CHF M2 — mechanically supporting the franc. Canada's CA improvement from oil (estimated +C$25-35bn cumulative) narrows the deficit but doesn't close it. The NET external balance for CAD/CHF is decisively CHF-positive: even with oil at $100+, Canada runs a deficit while Switzerland runs a 7% surplus. This is the single most powerful structural force pushing CAD/CHF lower over time. Additionally, if oil normalises toward $85-90 by Q4 (forward curve pricing), Canada's CA improvement stalls while Switzerland's surplus continues unabated.
key_bilateral_metrics: 6M cumulative Swiss CA surplus: ~CHF 35bn
6M cumulative Canadian CA: likely small deficit (even with oil)
Net structural flow advantage: strongly CHF-positive
Oil forward curve: $85-90 by Q4 2026 (forward pricing)
Gold trend: sustaining CHF refining flows above trend
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price oil normalisation but underweight the cumulative Swiss CA surplus effect on the cross.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral (too slow to dominate near-term), Strategic Bearish CAD/CHF (cumulative CA surplus flow overwhelms carry over 6M).

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: Canada is a net energy exporter; WTI at $104/bbl is a significant CAD positive via terms-of-trade improvement and CA deficit narrowing
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: Switzerland is the world's largest gold refiner (65-70% of global refining); gold at $4,700+ supports CHF via elevated refining flows running 25-30% above trend
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1: Oil ↑ $104/bbl → Canada CA deficit narrows from -1.1% to roughly -0.5% of GDP → modest CAD positive. Step 2: Gold ↑ $4,700+ → Swiss gold refining revenues elevated → modest CHF positive. Step 3: Net oil-vs-gold commodity impact: oil provides a larger CAD tailwind in absolute dollar terms than gold provides a CHF tailwind, giving a marginal net commodity edge to CAD. Step 4: However, the structural CA differential (-1.1% Canada vs +7.0% Switzerland) overwhelms the commodity price effect — Switzerland's 810bps CA advantage is not bridged by oil alone. Step 5: Net commodity exposure for the PAIR is approximately neutral-to-slight-CAD-positive on pure commodity price grounds, but the structural CA surplus of CHF 35bn/6M dominates at strategic horizons, producing a net CHF-positive external balance assessment.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil +10% to ~$114/bbl: CAD positive via improved CA and BoC hawkish repricing; CHF modestly negative (risk-on reduces safe-haven demand); net CAD/CHF positive, estimated +40-70 pips
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10% to ~$94/bbl: CAD negative via CA deterioration and BoC dovish repricing; CHF positive (risk-off + safe-haven demand); net CAD/CHF negative, estimated -60-100 pips
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: WTI crude oil is the dominant commodity driver for CAD/CHF, given Canada's direct exposure as a net energy exporter and oil's impact on the CA balance, while gold's effect on CHF is secondary and partially offsetting

tactical.bilateral_analysis: BoC guidance is hawkish-hold ('stand ready to respond as needed'), mildly CAD-supportive. SNB guidance is explicitly bearish CHF (intervention rhetoric: 'willingness to intervene has increased'). These offsetting signals produce a neutral net for CAD/CHF. BoC Governor Macklem stated that if oil prices continue to increase and remain elevated, 'there may be a need for consecutive increases in the policy rate.' This conditional hawkishness is CAD/CHF-positive but contingent on data. The SNB's Schlegel AGM speech reinforced 'unrestricted room for manoeuvre' — a cap on further CHF strength that limits CAD/CHF downside. The net effect is guidance-driven range compression: BoC hawkish hold provides a floor, SNB intervention rhetoric provides a ceiling.
key_bilateral_metrics: BoC April 29: hawkish hold, 'stand ready to respond'
SNB March 19: 'willingness to intervene has increased'
BoC next: June 10 (hold expected)
SNB next: June 18 (hold expected, intervention rhetoric)
Guidance net: offsetting
tactical.what_is_priced: Both central bank stances are well-priced. No bilateral guidance mispricing.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the guidance trajectory diverges slightly against CAD/CHF. The BoC's hawkish bias is conditional on CPI staying above 2.5% and USMCA not escalating — if USMCA worsens, Macklem explicitly signalled 'we may need to cut further,' which would be CAD/CHF-bearish. The SNB's intervention rhetoric is constrained by US Treasury currency-manipulator monitoring (Switzerland already meets 2 of 3 criteria), meaning the bark-vs-bite gap is meaningful. Over 6M, the SNB is more likely to maintain rhetoric than to actually intervene in size, limiting the effectiveness of guidance as a CHF-negative force. The BoC July 15 MPR (post-USMCA) is the dominant strategic guidance event for CAD; the SNB September 25 MPA is the key for CHF.
key_bilateral_metrics: BoC July 15 MPR: key post-USMCA guidance reset
SNB September 25 MPA: potential negative-rate signal
BoC conditional dovish risk: USMCA failure → cut signal
SNB intervention constraint: US Treasury monitoring
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets underweight BoC conditional dovish risk (USMCA failure scenario) and overweight SNB intervention efficacy.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral, Strategic Bearish. The divergence reflects the BoC's conditional dovish risk materialising over 6M if USMCA disappoints.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The pair faces two bilateral event risks that are both CAD/CHF-negative in expectation. First, the USMCA mandatory review deadline (July 1) creates pre-event risk premium for CAD — headline shock risk is elevated over the next 8 weeks, and any USMCA-negative development would weaken CAD (CAD/CHF lower) while CHF would benefit from safe-haven flows. Second, the active Iran ceasefire negotiation is binary: re-escalation strongly favours CHF (CAD/CHF lower) while ceasefire mildly favours CAD (CAD/CHF higher). The risk asymmetry is bearish CAD/CHF: the downside tail (re-escalation + USMCA negative) is larger than the upside tail (ceasefire + USMCA positive).
key_bilateral_metrics: USMCA deadline: July 1, 2026 (8 weeks away)
Iran ceasefire probability: ~50/50
USMCA probability mix: 10% renewal / 75% annual reviews / 15% withdrawal
Combined CAD/CHF downside tail probability: ~25-30%
Fed Chair transition May 15: USD vol → spillover to both legs
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price modest USMCA risk and balanced Iran outcome. The combined tail risk is underpriced for CAD/CHF.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the tail-risk distribution strongly favours CHF. Even if Iran resolves cleanly, the next 6 months include US-China tariff risks, European fiscal stress, and US debt ceiling — all of which activate CHF safe-haven flows. Canada additionally faces USMCA-specific risk that CHF is entirely insulated from. The cumulative probability of at least one major risk-off event is ~50% over 6M, each of which would push CAD/CHF lower.
key_bilateral_metrics: 6M cumulative major risk-off probability: ~50%
USMCA-specific risk: Canada only (CHF insulated)
Swiss-EU Bilaterals III: long-term positive for CHF (institutional anchor)
Canadian-specific event risk: USMCA + China FTA tariff threat
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets underweight cumulative tail-risk exposure. Modestly bearish CAD/CHF.

factors.horizon_split: Both Bearish; similar confidence. Event risk is persistently CAD/CHF-negative across horizons.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The fiscal differential is stark and unambiguously CHF-positive. Switzerland's federal debt/GDP at 16.1% is among the lowest in the developed world; Canada's at ~45-50% (federal) or ~80-85% (combined with provinces) is materially higher. Both carry AAA sovereign ratings, but Switzerland's fiscal trajectory (balanced budgets, debt brake) is structurally superior to Canada's ($50B+ annual deficits through 2031). The Carney government's fiscal expansion ($37.5B new spending over 5Y) marginally increases Canada's fiscal risk. During global fiscal stress episodes, the quality differential drives safe-asset flows toward Swiss government bonds (0.35% yield) and away from Canadian bonds (3.60% yield), compressing CAD/CHF.
key_bilateral_metrics: Canada federal debt/GDP: ~45-50% vs Switzerland: 16.1%
Canada 2025 deficit: $50B vs Switzerland 2025: +CHF 0.3B surplus
Sovereign ratings: both AAA across major agencies
Swiss 10Y: 0.35% vs Canada 10Y: 3.60% (safe-asset premium)
Fiscal trajectory: Canada deteriorating, Switzerland stable
tactical.what_is_priced: The fiscal gap is well-known and broadly priced. Where it's underpriced is tactical: in a fiscal-stress scenario, CHF attracts incremental safe-asset flows that compress CAD/CHF.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategic fiscal divergence widens further over 6 months as peer fiscal positions deteriorate. The quality differential between Swiss bonds and Canadian bonds is durable and compounds over time. Canada's fiscal trajectory continues to deteriorate with $50B+ deficits through 2031, while Switzerland maintains its debt brake framework and near-balanced budgets. Rating action risk is nil for both (both AAA stable), but the CDS spread differential (Switzerland ~10-15bps vs Canada ~25-30bps estimated) reflects the underlying quality gap that is likely to persist and potentially widen.
key_bilateral_metrics: 6M fiscal trajectory: Canada widening deficit; Switzerland balanced
Rating action risk: nil for both (both AAA stable)
CDS spreads: Switzerland ~10-15bps vs Canada ~25-30bps (estimated)
strategic.what_is_priced: Fiscal advantage well-priced but trajectory divergence underweighted.

factors.horizon_split: Both Bearish CAD/CHF at 75 confidence. Structural factor, stable signal.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The pair's technical structure is bearish. Spot at 0.5683 sits below all three MAs (50d 0.5730, 100d 0.5710, 200d 0.5723) in a bearishly stacked configuration. The bearish trend is currently described as 'very strong' for CAD/CHF by technical analysis sources. The descending channel from April 2025 remains intact. If CAD/CHF holds below 0.5750, the pair could be setting up for a pullback toward 0.5700 and possibly 0.5620. The recent sharp reversal from 0.5784 (April 28) to 0.5683 (May 9) — a decline of 100 pips in 10 days — confirms sellers dominate rallies. The October 2025 low at 0.5595 is the key downside target; a break below would open the door to 0.5500. Upside, the 200d SMA at 0.5723 is immediate resistance, with 0.5800 as the critical level that would negate the bearish structure.
key_bilateral_metrics: Resistance: 0.5723 (200d SMA), 0.5765 (descending channel), 0.5800
Recent price action: failed rally from 0.5784 → 0.5683 (bearish rejection)
Range percentile: 13th (near 52-week low)
tactical.what_is_priced: Bearish momentum is well-reflected in price. The risk is a consolidation bounce if the pair is oversold near 0.5660 support.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The descending channel from April 2025 defines the strategic trend: CAD/CHF has fallen from 0.6250 to 0.5683 in 14 months, a decline of approximately 9.1%. If the channel holds, the 6M target is approximately 0.5500-0.5550. The bearishly stacked MA configuration supports continued CHF strength bias. Strategic invalidation is a sustained break above 0.5800 (200d SMA + channel top confluence).
key_bilateral_metrics: 6M target range: 0.5500-0.5650
Strategic invalidation: sustained close above 0.5800
Descending channel slope: approximately -0.003/month
52-week range position: 13th percentile (bearish)
strategic.what_is_priced: Trend continuation is partially priced. The edge exists in the magnitude of decline being larger than forward curves imply.

factors.horizon_split: Both Bearish; tactical confidence slightly higher (55 vs 50) because near-term momentum is clearer.

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: CAD/CHF is a low-vol cross with estimated implied vol of 6-7% annualised and average daily range of 40-50 pips. The pair is in a slow-grind environment with episodic spikes around geopolitical events. Vol is neither elevated enough to make options expensive nor low enough to make them particularly attractive relative to their hedging value.
expression_assessment.directional_expression: Given the low-vol grind environment (estimated 6-7% IV), the preferred directional expression for a bearish CAD/CHF view is spot or forward rather than options. For bearish expression, a forward sale is optimal: it captures the pair's structural decline while forward points work in the seller's favour (selling CAD forward at a premium vs CHF). The optimal structure is short 3-6M CAD/CHF forward, capturing both directional decline and forward roll benefit. The 6M forward at 0.5617 with target 0.5550 captures 67 pips of expected edge over the forward-implied rate.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: Key events to consider for expression timing: Canada April CPI (May 19) creates a near-term vol spike risk — avoid initiating 1M forward sales immediately before the print; wait for post-CPI entry if the data confirms bearish direction. USMCA review (July 1) argues for positioning via 3M+ forwards that expire after the event, capturing the event premium. The SNB June 18 MPA is a binary CHF risk event — short-dated options may be warranted around that specific date.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: CAD/CHF is a less liquid cross than USD/CHF or USD/CAD — bid/offer spreads are typically 3-5 pips wider than USD crosses. Execution via interbank forward market is recommended over OTC options for size. Position sizing should account for gap risk given the thin open interest in the cross. The carry penalty for spot shorts (~10 pips/month) makes forward sales decisively more efficient than spot shorts for multi-week positions.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Non-USD cross; reduced relevance of dollar liquidity channel. Weight reduced -0.03 tactically (0.11 to 0.08) and -0.03 strategically (0.12 to 0.09).

tactical.bilateral_analysis: For this non-USD cross, the USD liquidity channel operates through the vehicle currency mechanism (most FX trades settle in USD). Both CAD and CHF have deep USD funding markets. CAD/USD cross-currency basis is typically tight; CHF/USD basis has widened modestly during the Iran war but remains inside crisis thresholds. The dollar smile places USD currently at the neutral bottom — neither strongly bid nor offered — which is neutral for CAD/CHF as neither leg benefits disproportionately. The Fed holding at 3.50-3.75% creates stable funding costs for both legs. No specific CAD/CHF basis data is available from public sources, but the cross trades through USD legs with adequate liquidity.
key_bilateral_metrics: Fed rate: 3.50-3.75% (held April 30)
DXY: 98.35 (neutral position on dollar smile)
CAD/USD basis: tight (no stress)
CHF/USD basis: modestly widened but not stressed
Cross-currency settlement: both via USD vehicle

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, Fed cuts (25-50bps) ease USD funding conditions for both legs roughly equally. The impact on CAD/CHF is indirect and approximately neutral — easier dollar conditions don't disproportionately favour either side of this cross. The dollar smile transition toward the bottom weakens USD broadly, supporting both CAD and CHF vs USD, but the net cross effect washes out.
key_bilateral_metrics: Expected Fed path: 1-2 cuts (3.25-3.50% by year-end)
Dollar smile effect on CAD/CHF: approximately neutral
Cross-basis trajectory: likely stable for both legs

factors.horizon_split: Both Neutral with low confidence. This factor is less relevant for non-USD crosses.

adjustments.reason: Pure risk-proxy pair (commodity vs safe-haven); risk factor deserves higher weight — tactical boosted from 0.10 to 0.13
Pure risk-proxy pair (commodity vs safe-haven); risk factor deserves higher weight — strategic boosted from 0.05 to 0.08
Non-USD cross; reduced relevance of dollar liquidity channel — tactical reduced from 0.11 to 0.08
Non-USD cross; reduced relevance of dollar liquidity channel — strategic reduced from 0.12 to 0.09

tactical.score_interpretation: Net score -0.130 falls in the -0.10 to -0.20 band, mapping to conviction 2/5 (low-conviction bearish). The single positive factor (Policy & Rates at +0.144) is overwhelmed by the cumulative negative factors: Inflation/Growth (-0.108), Risk Appetite (-0.078), Event Risk (-0.030), Fiscal (-0.030), and Market Structure (-0.028) collectively contribute -0.274.
tactical.rationale: The tactical bearish signal for CAD/CHF is driven by a fundamental asymmetry: the +225bps carry advantage for CAD is real but insufficient to overcome CHF's multi-pillar defensive advantages in the current stagflation/safe-haven regime. Factor 1 (Policy & Rates) is the sole bullish contributor at +0.144, reflecting the widest BoC-SNB gap in recent history, but the carry-to-vol ratio of +0.35 — while the best among CHF crosses — has been consistently overwhelmed by safe-haven flows since the Iran war began. Factor 2 (Inflation & Growth) at -0.108 reflects Switzerland's uniquely favourable inflation profile (0.6% headline, 0.3% core) creating a real-yield advantage that the market rewards with structural CHF demand, while Canada's stagflationary combination of high CPI (2.4%) and rising unemployment (6.7%) undermines the BoC's room to act. Factor 3 (Risk Appetite) at -0.078 captures the pair's high risk-beta: the Iran ceasefire binary creates asymmetric downside for CAD/CHF, and the USMCA pre-event risk premium adds CAD-specific headwind not shared by CHF. The three remaining negative factors (Event Risk, Fiscal, Market Structure) collectively contribute -0.088 of additional bearish pressure, with Fiscal at 75 confidence reflecting the unambiguous quality differential between Switzerland's 16.1% debt/GDP and Canada's 45-50% federal debt/GDP. The market is partially pricing this: the pair sits at the 13th percentile of its 52-week range, suggesting the bearish move is maturing. However, the price action in the most recent 10 sessions (rejection from 0.5784, decline to 0.5683) confirms sellers remain in control, and the bearishly stacked MA configuration (spot below all three MAs: 50d 0.5730, 100d 0.5710, 200d 0.5723) provides no technical cushion. The path of least resistance is lower toward 0.5620, with a test of the October 2025 low at 0.5595 possible on any risk-off catalyst. Key tactical catalysts: Canada April CPI (May 19), BoC Summary of Deliberations (mid-May), Iran ceasefire developments.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: CAD/CHF ranks as the 4th most attractive short among CHF crosses (after AUD/CHF, NOK/CHF, and GBP/CHF, which have worse carry-to-vol profiles for long positions). Among CAD crosses, short CAD/CHF is less attractive than short CAD/JPY (where carry works FOR the short position). The pair sits in the middle of the opportunity set: the carry penalty for being short CAD/CHF is the main drag on trade quality, requiring directional conviction to compensate.

conditions: Iran ceasefire remains unresolved
Oil holds $95-$105
BoC holds at June 10 with mildly hawkish tone
CHF safe-haven bid persists
base_case.path: The pair drifts lower through a series of lower highs and lower lows, testing 0.5650 in the first 10 days and potentially reaching 0.5620 by late May. The May 19 Canadian CPI release is the primary data catalyst: a hot print (2.7%) would provide a brief bounce toward 0.5720, while a soft print accelerates the decline toward 0.5600. Pullback zones for re-establishing shorts: 0.5720-0.5740 (50d SMA zone).

conditions: Iran ceasefire achieved with Hormuz reopening
Oil holds above $100 with safe-haven unwind
USMCA positive headline triggering CAD short squeeze
Iran re-escalation driving CHF safe-haven bid
USMCA negative headline weakening CAD
Weak Canadian data accelerating dovish BoC repricing

tactical.path_expectation: The pair is likely to drift lower over the next 2-4 weeks through a series of lower highs and lower lows, testing 0.5650 in the first 10 days and potentially reaching 0.5620 by late May. The May 19 Canadian CPI release is the primary data catalyst: a hot print (2.7%) would provide a brief bounce toward 0.5720, while a soft print (<2.3%) accelerates the decline toward 0.5600. Pullback zones for re-establishing shorts are 0.5720-0.5740 (50d SMA zone). The pattern shape is a grind lower with intermittent 30-40 pip bounces. The 200d SMA at 0.5723 and the descending channel top at 0.5765 cap any recovery attempts, while the 0.5595 October 2025 low anchors the downside on risk-off acceleration.

Increase to 4 if: Iran re-escalation confirmed AND CAD/CHF breaks 0.5595 (52-week low)

risk_reward.base_case_payoff: +38 pips net (+0.67%) after carry cost of ~10 pips
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Modestly favourable on expected value basis despite poor nominal risk/reward ratio of 0.36:1, because the carry cost is small relative to directional tail payoffs. The downside scenario's 30% probability and 133-pip payoff drives the positive expected value.

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score -0.259 is materially stronger than tactical (-0.130) because Factor 5 (External Balance) increases from tactical 0 to strategic -0.104 at its elevated weight (0.16), Factor 6 (Guidance) shifts from Neutral to Bearish (-0.066), and Factor 4 (Positioning) shifts from Neutral to mildly Bearish (-0.030). Only Factor 1 (Policy & Rates) remains positive at +0.132. The score maps to conviction 2/5 — the absolute magnitude is moderate but the signal is broad-based (9 of 10 factors are neutral or bearish for the pair).
strategic.rationale: The strategic case for lower CAD/CHF rests on the structural dominance of Switzerland's current-account surplus, fiscal quality, and low-inflation regime over Canada's carry advantage. Factor 5 (External Balance) at -0.104 is the dominant strategic contributor: the ~CHF 35bn of net Swiss inflows over 6 months, combined with a Canadian trade deficit even at elevated oil prices, creates persistent structural CAD/CHF selling pressure that compounds regardless of near-term tactical swings. Factor 1 (Policy & Rates) at +0.132 remains the sole bullish offset — the +225bps carry is real and compounds over time — but the carry regime has been consistently non-dominant since the Iran war shifted the market from carry-driven to safe-haven-driven, and this structural shift is unlikely to fully reverse over the 6M horizon even if Iran resolves. The key bilateral interaction is the oil-CHF tug of war: Canada's oil windfall supports CAD, but oil above $100 simultaneously signals geopolitical stress that supports CHF, partially cancelling the CAD benefit on this specific cross. Factor 6 (Guidance) at -0.066 reflects the conditional dovish risk from BoC if USMCA disappoints, combined with the limitation on SNB intervention effectiveness due to US Treasury currency-manipulator monitoring. Factor 8 (Fiscal) at -0.053 compounds as peer fiscal positions deteriorate while Switzerland remains pristine with a balanced budget and 16.1% debt/GDP. One statistical model projects a CAD/CHF average of approximately 0.58 by end-2026, which is above current spot and represents a bullish view. We disagree and expect the pair to trade toward 0.5500-0.5600 by Q4 2026. The carry cost of being short CAD/CHF is the primary friction: at +225bps annualised, a 6M short incurs approximately 65 pips of negative carry, meaning the pair must decline by more than 65 pips for the trade to be profitable. Our base case target of 0.5550 implies a 133-pip decline, comfortably exceeding the carry cost.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: CAD/CHF ranks as a moderate-conviction short in the CHF cross complex. Short AUD/CHF and NOK/CHF are less attractive (higher carry costs and hawkish central banks), while short NZD/CHF and EUR/CHF offer comparable or better risk/reward. Among CAD crosses, short CAD/CHF is the second most attractive after short CAD/JPY (where the carry works against the long position). The strategic case is most compelling via forward sale structure to avoid the negative carry drag on a spot short position.

conditions: USMCA slides to annual reviews without full tariff rollback
Oil normalises to $85-95 by Q4 as Iran risk premium unwinds partially
BoC holds at 2.25% with Swiss CA surplus of ~CHF 35bn compounding over 6M
SNB holds at 0.00% with intervention rhetoric limiting but not reversing CHF appreciation
base_case.path: Phase 1 (May-July): range 0.5550-0.5750 with downward bias; USMCA pre-event risk compresses the pair. Phase 2 (July-August): post-USMCA resolution — if annual reviews (75% probability), modest CAD relief rally to 0.5700 area before CHF structural forces reassert. Phase 3 (August-Q4): renewed CHF strength on autumn risk windows (US debt ceiling, European fiscal), oil normalisation, and Swiss CA surplus accumulation drives pair toward 0.5550. Pullback levels for adding shorts: 0.5730-0.5780 (MA cluster). Pattern: stepped decline with 50-80 pip bounces every 4-6 weeks.

conditions: Iran ceasefire with Hormuz reopening sustained above $100 oil with no safe-haven unwind
USMCA renewal with full tariff rollback triggering violent CAD short unwind
BoC hawkish pivot with rate hike signal as CPI persists above 2.5%
Iran re-escalation maximising CHF safe-haven demand
USMCA failure forcing BoC to signal rate cuts
Global recession fears compressing commodity currencies with CHF as maximum safe-haven beneficiary

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path is likely a grind lower interrupted by carry-driven bounces. Phase 1 (May-July): range 0.5550-0.5750 with downward bias; USMCA pre-event risk compresses the pair toward the lower end. Phase 2 (July-August): post-USMCA resolution — if annual reviews (75% probability), modest CAD relief rally to the 0.5700 area before CHF structural forces reassert as Swiss CA surplus accumulation resumes. Phase 3 (August-Q4): renewed CHF strength on autumn risk windows (US debt ceiling, European fiscal stress), oil normalisation toward $85-90 per the forward curve, and continued Swiss CA surplus accumulation drives the pair toward 0.5550. Key pullback levels for adding short positions are 0.5730-0.5780 (MA cluster and channel resistance). The pattern is a stepped decline with 50-80 pip bounces every 4-6 weeks as carry longs periodically reassert before being overwhelmed by the structural CHF bid.

Increase to 4 if: USMCA failure confirmed AND BoC forced to signal rate cuts AND oil normalises below $90

risk_reward.base_case_payoff: +43 pips net (+0.76%) over 6M after carry cost of 65 pips
risk_reward.downside_case_loss: -257 pips net (-4.52%) including carry cost over 6M
risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: probability-weighted expected value positive at +25 pips
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Roughly balanced distribution with modest positive expected value. The carry cost is the main drag; without it, R/R would be materially better. Recommendation: express via forward sale at 0.5617 (6M forward) with target 0.5550, capturing 67 pips of forward edge — this is the most efficient strategic expression.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: One statistical model projects a CAD/CHF average of approximately 0.58 by end-2026; the broader Canadian dollar consensus expects 'gradual CAD recovery expected later in 2026'; National Bank of Canada sees 'scope for renewed CAD depreciation through quarter-end' but a 'more constructive view for H2 2026' [Sources: Traders Union model, MTFX Group, National Bank of Canada]
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: approximately 100-130 pips below consensus end-2026 forecast of 0.58+
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We disagree with the bullish CAD/CHF consensus for three reasons. First, the structural Swiss current-account surplus (~CHF 35bn over 6M, equivalent to ~10% of CHF M2) generates persistent CHF demand that statistical models based on rate differentials and PPP reversion fail to capture — this is Factor 5 (External Balance) which carries 0.16 weight at the strategic horizon and contributes -0.104 to our score. Second, the carry advantage is being eroded by the safe-haven regime shift induced by the Iran war, which we believe is more persistent than consensus assumes; carry-trade strategies in CHF pairs have been consistently unprofitable since the conflict began, and even a ceasefire does not immediately restore the pre-war carry regime. Third, the USMCA risk creates a CAD-specific headwind (15% withdrawal probability, 75% annual-reviews probability with embedded uncertainty) that peer currency models using only macro variables do not price into the CHF cross, generating a systematic bias toward underestimating CAD vulnerability on this specific pair.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: +100-130 pips vs consensus, approximately +1.8-2.3% on a 6M forward sale
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: -200-250 pips vs target if CAD/CHF rallies to 0.58+ on Iran resolution + USMCA renewal

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical and strategic views are aligned (both Bearish CAD/CHF, conviction 2/5). The strategic score is more negative (-0.259 vs -0.130) primarily because the External Balance factor's 0.16 weight contributes -0.104 at the strategic horizon versus 0 tactically, and because Guidance and Positioning shift from Neutral to Bearish over the longer horizon. This alignment simplifies implementation: short CAD/CHF positions initiated tactically can be held through the strategic horizon without horizon-mismatch risk.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: The carry cost over 6 months (~65 pips) requires discipline — position must be managed with defined stop levels (0.5800 for full exit) rather than held passively. Preferred expression is 3-6M forward sale to capture forward premium; spot short incurs negative carry drag of ~10 pips/month.

forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: Forward 0.5649 vs expected 0.5600 = +49 pip edge for short CAD/CHF forward
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: Forward 0.5617 vs expected 0.5550 = +67 pip edge for short CAD/CHF forward

catalysts.event: BoC Summary of Governing Council Deliberations
catalysts.rationale: May surface hawkish dissent not visible in the headline statement. Any 'some members favoured a hike' language would lift CAD short rates and support CAD/CHF through hawkish BoC repricing. This is a limited but meaningful near-term catalyst given the conditional nature of BoC hawkishness (oil-dependent). The bilateral transmission is via CAD rate repricing → CAD strengthens → pair rises.

Carbon-levy base effects should push headline above 2.5%. A hot print (2.7%) is CAD/CHF-bullish via hawkish BoC repricing — markets would bring forward the next BoC meeting as a live event. A soft print (<2.3%) is bearish via dovish repricing as it reduces the justification for BoC's hawkish-hold stance. The bilateral transmission to CHF is indirect: CHF does not directly react to Canadian CPI but benefits asymmetrically if the soft print triggers risk-off sentiment about Canadian stagflation.

Q1 expected to show stalling growth after Q4 -0.6% contraction. A below-consensus print (below 0.5% annualised) would be CAD/CHF-negative as it raises BoC dovish-pivot risk and signals that the mortgage renewal wave is beginning to compress consumer spending. The bilateral transmission is CAD-specific: CHF would remain well-bid as a safe haven while CAD weakens on growth disappointment, pushing the pair lower.

Hold at 2.25% is fully priced by markets. The statement tone is the trade — hawkish language (explicit reference to potential hike if CPI stays above 2.5%) supports CAD/CHF; any dovish shift referencing USMCA risk or growth weakness weighs on CAD and compresses the pair. The bilateral transmission is rate-differential driven: CAD is sensitive to BoC tone while CHF is largely insulated from Canadian monetary policy signalling.

Hold at 0.00% is expected. Escalation of intervention rhetoric could temporarily cap CHF strength and provide a relief bounce for CAD/CHF. The binary risk is a surprise cut to -0.25% (approximately 15% probability) which would be strongly CAD/CHF-bullish as it widens the carry differential to +250bps and signals SNB intent to actively resist CHF appreciation. Conversely, if inflation undershoots further and the SNB signals next-step easing, CHF could strengthen despite the hold, pushing the pair lower.

Warsh's first Summary of Economic Projections sets the tone for H2 Fed policy. A dovish dot plot (2 cuts) weakens USD broadly, mildly supporting both CAD and CHF — net CAD/CHF neutral. A hawkish surprise (no cuts) strengthens USD, weakening both legs — also approximately neutral for the cross. The bilateral transmission is via the USD vehicle: both CAD and CHF are negatively correlated with USD, so directional USD moves largely cancel out in the cross.

The most important bilateral catalyst for CAD/CHF. USMCA failure (15% probability) would be strongly CAD/CHF-bearish as CAD weakens on trade disruption AND CHF strengthens on safe-haven flows — a double hit to the pair. Even the base case (75% annual reviews) generates pre-event CAD weakness as markets price in uncertainty. CHF is entirely insulated from USMCA, making this a pure CAD-negative event that widens the bilateral divergence. The bilateral transmission is asymmetric: CAD is the only affected leg, amplifying the downward pressure on the cross.

The first post-USMCA Monetary Policy Report incorporating trade-policy reality into BoC's formal projections. A hawkish bias is likely if CPI has been sustained above 2.5% through May-June; a dovish pivot is possible if USMCA disappointment prompts downward growth revisions. This is the key event for setting H2 2026 CAD trajectory and determining whether the carry advantage remains intact or begins to compress. The bilateral transmission is CAD rate path: a dovish MPR would compress the +225bps differential and reduce the sole bullish factor in our framework.

invalidators.condition: CAD/CHF closes above 0.5800 for 5 consecutive days
invalidators.mechanism: A sustained break above 0.5800 would break the descending channel from April 2025, challenge the bearishly stacked MA configuration (50d, 100d, 200d all below 0.5800), and trigger systematic trend-following algorithms to flip to pair-neutral. This would signal that the structural CHF bid has been overcome by carry and/or oil tailwinds.

invalidators.condition: WTI crude sustains above $110/bbl for 10+ days while Iran ceasefire is confirmed
invalidators.mechanism: Sustained oil above $110 would improve Canada's CA balance by an additional 0.5-1.0% of GDP, reinforcing carry with terms-of-trade support. A simultaneous Iran ceasefire would unwind CHF safe-haven premium, removing the main force suppressing carry-trade returns. The combined effect would likely push CAD/CHF toward the 0.5800-0.5850 range, negating the bearish thesis.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: WTI front-month price + Iran ceasefire headline confirmation

invalidators.condition: SNB cuts to -0.25% AND announces intervention programme of CHF 50bn/quarter
invalidators.mechanism: Combined SNB policy action (negative rates + large-scale intervention) would historically erode CHF strength by widening the carry differential to +250bps at the policy rate while simultaneously reducing CHF supply through FX purchases. This would remove the structural CHF bid and shift the pair toward its PPP-fair-value direction. Sight deposit spikes of CHF 30bn+ per week would confirm active intervention.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SNB MPA announcements (June 18, September 25) + weekly sight deposit data at data.snb.ch

invalidators.condition: Canadian April CPI prints above 3.0% triggering explicit BoC hike signal at June 10 meeting
invalidators.mechanism: A CPI print above 3.0% would force BoC to signal a hike, potentially widening the carry differential to +250-275bps at the policy rate. This would shift Factor 1 (Policy & Rates) to high-confidence Bullish and partially offset the bearish factors. The bilateral transmission is CAD short-rate repricing → higher short rates → carry becomes dominant vs safe-haven flows.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Statistics Canada CPI release (May 19) + BoC statement language

invalidators.condition: USMCA renewed with full tariff rollback announced before July 1 deadline
invalidators.mechanism: A full USMCA renewal would collapse the CAD-specific trade risk premium (estimated 100-150 pips embedded in CAD crosses), triggering a violent CAD rally across all crosses including CAD/CHF. The bilateral transmission is purely CAD-positive with no CHF offset — CHF would actually weaken modestly on risk-on from the North American trade stability, producing a double lift for CAD/CHF.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: USMCA review headlines from Reuters, AP, Global Affairs Canada

signposts.confirms_if: Oil normalises below $95 (CAD tailwind weakens, reducing the sole major bullish factor for the pair)
signposts.invalidates_if: Oil sustains above $110 with Iran ceasefire simultaneously confirmed (carry overwhelms safe-haven)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Oil is the primary bilateral differentiator for this cross: CAD is directly exposed as a net energy exporter while CHF is energy-neutral. Crucially, oil above $100 simultaneously supports CAD via terms-of-trade AND supports CHF via geopolitical safe-haven signalling, making the net CAD/CHF impact more ambiguous than for USD/CAD. This dual-channel effect is unique to the CAD/CHF cross.

signposts.confirms_if: Stays 0.3-0.8% (real-yield premium intact; CHF structural demand persists via low-inflation feedback loop)
signposts.invalidates_if: Rises above 1.5% for two consecutive months (eliminates Switzerland's uniquely low inflation advantage, reducing CHF structural demand)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Switzerland's uniquely low inflation (0.6% vs Canada's 2.4%) is the core of the bilateral real-yield differential and the self-reinforcing disinflationary feedback loop (CHF strength → import deflation → lower CPI → more real-yield support → more CHF demand). This indicator is more important for CAD/CHF than for other CHF crosses because the 180bps inflation gap is the widest bilateral contrast in the pair analysis.

signposts.confirms_if: Spot remains below 50d SMA (bearish MA configuration intact; trend continuation confirmed)
signposts.invalidates_if: Spot closes above 50d SMA for 5 consecutive days (dynamic resistance broken; trend regime change signalled)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The 50d SMA has acted as dynamic resistance for the entire 2026 decline, with the most recent rejection at 0.5784 (April 28-29) confirming its role as a ceiling. A break above signals the carry advantage is reasserting over safe-haven flows — the most important short-term technical signal for this specific pair given the slow-grind, low-vol environment.

signposts.current_level: Stable through Q1 2026 (precise weekly data not publicly available in real-time)
signposts.confirms_if: Stable or mild rise (less than CHF 10bn week-on-week) confirms no active large-scale intervention, leaving CHF appreciation trend intact
signposts.invalidates_if: Spikes CHF 30bn or more in any single week (active large-scale SNB intervention confirmed, historically associated with CHF weakness and CAD/CHF recovery)
signposts.bilateral_importance: SNB sight deposits are the most direct proxy for active FX intervention by the SNB. The SNB's intervention rhetoric ('willingness to intervene has increased') is a key factor limiting the pair's downside, but the difference between rhetoric and action is precisely what sight deposits reveal. For CAD/CHF, SNB intervention is one of the only mechanisms that could rapidly reverse the structural CHF appreciation trend.

signposts.current_level: 35% US tariff on non-USMCA goods; mandatory review deadline July 1, 2026
signposts.confirms_if: USMCA escalation rhetoric or withdrawal threat (CAD-negative; CHF safe-haven positive — pair falls)
signposts.invalidates_if: Renewal announcement or deadline extension (collapses CAD risk premium; pair could rally 100-150 pips rapidly)
signposts.bilateral_importance: USMCA is a CAD-specific risk that CHF is entirely insulated from, making it the purest bilateral event catalyst in this pair. Canada sends approximately 75-80% of exports to the US under USMCA; a tariff disruption would materially weaken Canada's terms of trade and growth outlook without any corresponding negative for Switzerland. This asymmetric exposure makes USMCA the single most important political signpost for CAD/CHF specifically.

assumptions: Forward points estimated from the +225bps policy rate differential; actual interbank forward rates may differ due to basis and bid/offer spreads not available from public sources.

Implied volatility for CAD/CHF estimated from component-pair IVs (USD/CAD ~7%, EUR/CHF ~7-8%) via triangulation; no direct CAD/CHF options surface data found from public sources — estimated IV of 6-7% may differ from actual.
Cross-currency basis for CAD/CHF not found from public sources; assumed tight based on both legs having deep USD funding markets.

Weight adjustment: Risk Appetite weight boosted +0.03 (from 0.10 to 0.13 tactical; 0.05 to 0.08 strategic) because CAD/CHF is a pure risk-proxy pair (commodity vs safe-haven). USD Liquidity weight reduced by -0.03 (from 0.11 to 0.08 tactical; 0.12 to 0.09 strategic) because this is a non-USD cross with limited direct dollar liquidity channel.

PPP fair value of ~0.81 for CAD/CHF is drawn from OECD data cited in both dossiers; the pair has been below PPP for over a decade, limiting PPP's tactical utility and making it a strategic floor indicator rather than a reversion target.

Swiss bilateral trade data (CHF 7.6bn imports from Canada, CHF 4bn exports to Canada in 2023) is the latest publicly available; 2025/2026 data estimated to have grown modestly given gold and pharma export trends.

CFTC positioning percentiles are calculated vs 5-year history for CHF (95th percentile for asset managers) and vs 1-year range for CAD (70th percentile for leveraged funds) — different reference periods may affect the relative squeeze analysis.
Iran ceasefire probability of ~50/50 is based on market pricing inference rather than direct probability surveys; actual probability distribution may differ materially from this assumption.

USMCA probability distribution (10% renewal, 75% annual reviews, 15% withdrawal) is based on analyst consensus from cited sources; actual outcome distribution is highly uncertain and path-dependent on US political developments.
Spot rate of 0.5683 is as of May 9, 2026 close; the analysis date noted in the report header is May 6, 2026 — a minor discrepancy acknowledged but immaterial to the analytical conclusions.
